personas.name: Client Library Author
personas.role: Implementing an SDK against the wire protocol

stories.text: As a client library author, I want a fixed-size frame header so that I can read exactly N bytes to determine the frame type and payload length without buffering variable-length prefixes.
As a client library author, I want unknown frame types to be safely skippable by reading the length prefix so that my SDK works against newer server versions without crashing.
As a client library author, I want the codec to round-trip every frame type deterministically so that I can write encode/decode tests that compare byte sequences across SDK languages.
As a client library author, I want causal context to be a structured field in the message envelope so that I can extract ordering information without parsing the application payload.
As a client library author, I want stream IDs in the frame header so that I can demultiplex frames to the correct conversation or subscription handler without inspecting the payload.

personas.name: Application Developer
personas.role: Building messaging applications using a Liminal client SDK

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to open multiple subscriptions over a single connection so that I do not need to manage a connection pool for different channels.

As an application developer, I want to receive explicit Accept, Defer, or Reject signals for my published messages so that I can adapt my publish rate without guessing whether the consumer is keeping up.
As an application developer, I want the connection handshake to negotiate the protocol version automatically so that I do not need to configure version compatibility manually.
As an application developer, I want schema negotiation to happen during subscription so that I learn about type mismatches before publishing, not after.
As an application developer, I want conversation lifecycle frames (open, message, close, error) so that I can track multi-step exchanges without building correlation logic in my application code.
As an operator, I want every protocol error to produce an explicit error frame with a numeric reason code so that I can monitor error rates and categorize failures without parsing log messages.
As an operator, I want the connection state machine to reject out-of-sequence frames so that misbehaving clients are detected and disconnected rather than silently corrupting bus state.
As an operator, I want per-stream backpressure state so that I can identify which specific subscriptions are under pressure without aggregating across the entire connection.
As an operator, I want Ping/Pong keepalive frames so that dead connections are detected within a configurable timeout rather than lingering until the OS TCP timeout fires.
